name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّتِى تُجَـٰدِلُكَ فِى زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَآ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (١)
```

```english
Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah. Allah hears the conversation between the two of you. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (1)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِهِم مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِهِمْ ۖ إِنْ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى وَلَدْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (٢)
```

```english
As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who bore them, and indeed they utter an outrage and a lie. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (2)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا۟ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٣)
```

```english
Those who repudiate their wives by ³ih¡r and then retract what they have said, shall set free a slave before they may touch each other. This you are advised [to carry out], and Allah is well aware of what you do. (3)
```

```arabic
فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعَامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِينًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤)
```

```english
He who cannot afford [to free a slave] shall fast for two successive months before they may touch each other. If he cannot [do so], he shall feed sixty needy persons. This, that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle. These are Allah’s bounds, and there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (4)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ كُبِتُوا۟ كَمَا كُبِتَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٥)
```

```english
Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle will be subdued just as those who passed before them were subdued. We have certainly sent down manifest signs, and there is a humiliating punishment for the faithless. (5)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ ۚ أَحْصَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ وَنَسُوهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٦)
```

```english
The day when Allah will raise them all together, He will inform them about what they have done. Allah has kept account of it, while they have forgotten, and Allah is witness to all things. (6)
```

## (Commentary)

The surah addresses various themes including legal rulings, etiquette, and descriptions. One part deals with the rulings of *zihar* (الظهار) — *a pre-Islamic form of divorce where a husband likens his wife to his mother's back* — and secret counsel (النجوى), as well as the etiquette of sitting in assemblies. Another part describes the state of those who defy Allah and His Messenger, and those who befriend the enemies of the religion (الدين). It also describes the believers who refrain from befriending them, promising them a beautiful promise in this world and the Hereafter.

The surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما** **"Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah, and Allah hears your conversation"** (58:1) etc. It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘*: **الاشتكاء** (complaining) is to manifest the distress one feels within oneself, while **الشكاية** (complaint) is to manifest the distress caused by another. He said: **التحاور** (conversation) is the back-and-forth exchange of speech; it is said: "he conversed (حاوره) with him," meaning he exchanged words with him, and "they conversed (تحاورا)." End quote.

The first four or six verses were revealed regarding *zihar* (الظهار). This was a type of divorce among the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). A man would say to his wife: "You are to me like the back of my mother," whereby she would be separated from him and become permanently forbidden to him. One of the *Ansar* (الأنصار) — *the Helpers of Medina* — performed *zihar* against his wife and then regretted it. His wife came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to question him about it, hoping to find a way to return to him, pleading (تُجادِلُكَ) with him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding that and complaining to Allah; thus, the verses were revealed.

The meaning of "hearing" (السمع) in His saying: **قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ** "Allah has certainly heard" is the answering of the prayer and the fulfillment of the need, by way of metonymy (الكناية), which is common. The evidence for this is His saying: **تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللهِ** "who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah," which indicates that she was seeking a way not to be separated from her husband. As for His saying: **وَاللهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما** "and Allah hears your conversation," the "hearing" here is in its well-known literal sense.

The meaning is: Allah has indeed responded to the woman who pleads with you regarding her husband — who had performed *zihar* against her — and who complains of her grief and the ill state that has befallen her to Allah; and Allah hears your exchange of words, for Allah is All-Hearing (سميع) of sounds, All-Seeing (بصير) of all that is visible.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ** **"As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who gave birth to them"** (58:2) etc. This is a negation of the ruling of *zihar* known among them and an annulment of its effect regarding divorce and permanent prohibition, by negating the motherhood of the wife to the husband through *zihar*. For the custom of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) would equate the wife with the mother because of *zihar*, making her as permanently forbidden to her husband as a mother is to her son.

Thus, His saying: **ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ** "they are not their mothers" means according to the consideration of the Law (الشرع), such that they would be legally equated with them due to *zihar* and thus become permanently forbidden to them. He then emphasized this by His saying: **إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ** "their mothers are only those who gave birth to them," meaning the mothers of the husbands are none other than the women who gave birth to them.

He then emphasized this a second time by His saying: **وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنْكَراً مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَزُوراً** **"Indeed they utter an abominable and lying word"** (58:2), with a context of emphasis. That is, these husbands who perform *zihar* utter, through this act, an abominable (منكراً) word that the Law (الشرع) denounces, as it has neither recognized nor legislated it; and a lie (زوراً) in the sense that it does not correspond to the Law, just as it does not correspond to the external reality of existence. Thus, the verse informs us that *zihar* does not effect a divorce. This does not contradict the obligation of atonement (الكفارة) upon him if he intends to have intercourse after *zihar*; the marriage remains intact, though intercourse is forbidden before the atonement.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ** **"Indeed Allah is All-excusing, All-forgiving"** (58:2) is not devoid of an indication that it is a forgiven sin. However, the mention of atonement (الكفارة) in the following verse, concluded by His saying: **وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** "These are the bounds of Allah, and for the faithless there is a painful punishment," perhaps indicates that forgiveness is conditional upon the atonement.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا** **"Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said, shall set free a slave before they touch each other"** (58:3) etc. The speech is in the sense of a condition, which is why the *fa* (الفاء) entered the predicate (الخبر) because it carries the meaning of a recompense (الجزاء). The gist is: those who have performed *zihar* and then intend to return to what they said, must manumit a slave.

In His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا** "before they touch each other," there is an indication that the ruling in the verse applies to one who performed *zihar* and then intended to return to the state he was in before the *zihar*. This serves as a context (قرينة) that the meaning of **يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا** "retract what they have said" is the intention to return by undoing what they had affirmed through *zihar*.

The meaning is: those who perform *zihar* against their wives and then intend to return to the word of *zihar* they uttered by undoing it through intercourse, must manumit a slave before they touch each other.

It has been said that the meaning of their "returning to what they said" is their regret over the *zihar*. However, regret can be the resultant meaning, but not the literal meaning of the phrase **يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا**.

It has also been said that the meaning of their "returning to what they said" is their returning to the utterance of the word of *zihar* by pronouncing it a second time. The problem with this is that it would necessitate that the atonement (الكفارة) is always contingent upon a second *zihar* rather than the first, and the verse does not imply this. Furthermore, the Prophetic Tradition (السنة) only considers the occurrence of *zihar* itself, regardless of its repetition.

He then concluded the verse with His saying: **ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ** **"This is what you are advised with, and Allah is well aware of what you do"** (58:3), as a notification that the atonement He commanded is an exhortation from Him based on His awareness of that act of theirs. Thus, the atonement is that by which the consequence of the act is removed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا** **"But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast for two successive months before they touch each other"** (58:4) until the end of the verse. This is the second option of atonement (الكفارة), contingent upon the first option for one who is unable to perform it; it is the fasting of two consecutive months before they touch each other. He restricted it again with the phrase **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا** "before they touch each other" to dispel the illusion that the restriction applied only to the first option.

His saying: **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً** **"and he who is not able [to fast] shall feed sixty needy persons"** (58:4) is an explanation of the third option. Whoever cannot endure the fasting of two consecutive months must feed sixty needy persons. The detailed discussion of all this belongs to Jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying: **ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** **"This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle"** (58:4), meaning: what We have established of the ruling and what We have obligated of the atonement — whereby We maintained the bond of marriage and imposed the atonement for whoever intends to return to intercourse as a recompense for practicing a custom from the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — all of this is so that you may believe in Allah and His Messenger and reject the falsehoods of those customs.

His saying: **وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** **"These are the bounds of Allah, and for the faithless there is a painful punishment"** (58:4). The **حد** (bound/limit) of a thing is that where it ends and is not to be exceeded; its root meaning is "prevention." The meaning is that the options We have obligated or the rulings We establish are the bounds (حدود) of Allah, so do not exceed them through disobedience. For those who disbelieve in what We have judged regarding *zihar* or in the laws We have legislated — by way of opposition and defiance — there is a painful punishment.

It appears that "disbelief" (الكفر) here refers to the rejection of the ruling and the adherence to *zihar* as an effective and accepted custom. This is supported by His saying: **ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ** "This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle," meaning that you should submit to the fact that Allah's judgment is the truth and that His Messenger is truthful and trustworthy in conveying it. He emphasized this with His saying: **وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ** "These are the bounds of Allah" etc. It is also possible that "disbelief" refers to disbelief in the realm of action, which is disobedience (العصيان).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحَادُّونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ كُبِتُوا كَما كُبِتَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** **"Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle shall be confounded, just as those who were before them were confounded"** (58:5) etc. **المحادة** (opposition) is to hinder and defy, and **الكبت** (confounding) is to humiliate and disgrace.

Although this verse and the one following it could be a new sentence (استئناف) explaining the matter of opposing Allah and His Messenger in terms of its consequence and effect, the apparent context suggests they are intended to provide the reason for the conclusion of the previous verse, which carries the meaning of a prohibition against opposing Allah and His Messenger. The meaning is: We have only commanded you to have faith in Allah and His Messenger and prohibited you from exceeding the bounds of Allah and disbelieving in them because those who oppose Allah and His Messenger through defiance are humiliated and disgraced just as those before them were humiliated and disgraced.

He then emphasized this by His saying: **وَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** **"We have certainly sent down manifest signs, and for the faithless there is a humiliating punishment"** (58:5). That is, there is no doubt that they are from Us and that Our Messenger is truthful and trustworthy in conveying them; and for those who disbelieve in them and reject them, there is a humiliating and disgraceful punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا** **"On the day when Allah will raise them all together and inform them of what they did"** (58:6). This is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for His saying: **وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ** "and for the faithless there is a painful punishment." That is, for them is the painful punishment on the day Allah raises them, which is the Day of Reckoning and Recompense, and He will inform them of the reality of all they did in the world.

His saying: **أَحْصاهُ اللهُ وَنَسُوهُ** **"Allah has kept its account, while they have forgotten it"** (58:6). **الإحصاء** (keeping account) is to encompass the number of a thing without anything being missed. Al-Raghib said: *al-ihsa’* (الإحصاء) is to obtain by number; it is said: "I counted (أحصيت) such and such," and it is derived from the word for pebbles (الحصى). Its use in this sense stems from the fact that they used to rely on pebbles for counting, just as we rely on fingers. End quote.

His saying: **وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** **"and Allah is witness to all things"** (58:6) is the reason for His saying: **أَحْصاهُ اللهُ** "Allah has kept its account." The explanation of Allah's being a witness (شهادة) over all things has already been mentioned at the end of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from ‘A’ishah, who said: "Blessed is He whose hearing encompasses all things. I was hearing the speech of Khawlah bint Tha‘labah, though some of it was hidden from me, while she was complaining about her husband to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, saying: 'O Messenger of Allah, he consumed my youth and I spread my womb for him — until when I became old and my childbearing ceased, he performed *zihar* against me. O Allah, I complain to You.' She did not leave until Gabriel descended with these verses: **قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها** 'Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband' — and he was Aws ibn al-Samit."

**I say:** The narrations from Sunni sources regarding this meaning are very numerous. They differ regarding the name of the woman, her father's name, her husband's name, and his father's name. The most well-known is that her name was Khawlah bint Tha‘labah and her husband's name was Aws ibn al-Samit al-Ansari. Al-Qummi mentioned the summary of the story in a narration, and he has another narration that will follow.

In *al-Majma‘*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ـ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا** "Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said." As for what the Leaders of Guidance (أئمة الهدى) — from the Progeny of Muhammad عليه‌السلام — held, it is that the meaning of "returning" (العود) is the intention for intercourse — and the undoing of the word he uttered — for intercourse is not permissible for him except after the atonement (الكفارة), and the ruling of his first word is not annulled except after the atonement.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn Abi ‘Abdillah narrated to us, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Abu Wallad, from Humran, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "A woman from the Muslims came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, so-and-so is my husband — and I have spread my womb for him and assisted him in his worldly and otherworldly affairs — he has seen nothing disliked from me, yet I complain of him to you.' He said: 'Regarding what do you complain of him?' She said: 'He said: "You are forbidden to me like the back of my mother" — and he has turned me out of my house, so look into my matter.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to her: 'Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has not sent down a book in which I may judge between you and your husband — and I dislike being among those who are pretentious (المتكلفين).' So she began to weep and complain of her state to Allah عز وجل and to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and she turned to leave."

He said: "Then Allah, Blessed and Exalted, heard her pleading with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding her husband and what she complained to him, and Allah sent down Quran regarding that: **بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ ، قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها** — until His saying — **وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ**."

He said: "So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent for the woman, and she came to him. He said to her: 'Bring your husband.' She brought him, and he said to him: 'Did you say to this wife of yours: "You are forbidden to me like the back of my mother"?' He said: 'I did say that to her.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: 'Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has sent down Quran regarding you and your wife' — and he recited: **بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ ـ قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ** — until His saying — **إِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ**. 'So take your wife back to you, for you have uttered an abominable and lying word, and Allah has excused you and forgiven you; do not repeat it.'"

He said: "The man left, regretting what he had said to his wife. Allah عز وجل disliked that for the believers thereafter and Allah sent down: **الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا** 'Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said,' meaning what the man said to his wife: 'You are to me like the back of my mother.'"

He said: "So whoever says it after Allah has excused and forgiven the first man, then upon him is **فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا** 'the setting free of a slave before they touch each other,' meaning intercourse. **ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ ـ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا ـ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً** 'This is what you are advised with, and Allah is well aware of what you do. But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast for two successive months before they touch each other; and he who is not able [to fast] shall feed sixty needy persons.'" He said: "So Allah made this the punishment for whoever performs *zihar* after the prohibition. Then He said: **ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ** 'This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle. These are the bounds of Allah.' He said: 'This is the bound (حد) of *zihar*.'" The tradition continues.

**I say:** The verse, given its context and especially the mention of excuse and forgiveness at its end, is more closely applicable to the story as narrated in this tradition. There is also no problem with it in terms of its chain of transmission (السند), except that it does not fully align with the literal sense of the verse's saying: **الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا** "Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said."
